msgs.text:
Turn 21 — compactness audit: Turn 20 needs a cofinal finite-quotient repair I re-read the three current attachments only. The problem is still the over-G isomorphism question for ΓS,ΓH, with F=P⋊G, P the free G-operator pro-2 group on x0,x1, and the relators rS,rH as in the problem statement. The requested output is an isomorphism Φ:ΓS→ΓH satisfying πH∘Φ=πS.

The result of this turn is:

Turn 20’s conclusion survives, but its “finite P/PN ” formulation must be corrected.The correction is structural, not cosmetic.

1. The issue in Turn 20

Turn 20 defined P=P1⊃P2 ⊃⋯ as a complete G-stable Magnus/Zassenhaus filtration and then asserted that each P/PN is a finite 2-group.That cannot be right in the G-operator setting. Already on the first layer, Turn 1 computedPab≅ΛX0⊕ΛX1,Λ=Z2 [[G]],and the abelianized relator rows live over Λ, not over a finite ring.

Since Λ=Z2 [[G]] is generally infinite, the degree-only quotient P/P2, and hence usually P/PN, is profinite but not finite.

So the sentenceSN is finite because P/PN is finite has to be replaced.This does not reopen the Magnus obstruction tower. It only changes the compactness indexing category.

2. Correct finite quotients: truncate both degree and coefficients The right cofinal system is two-parameter.LetM=Z2 [[G]]⟨⟨X0,X1⟩⟩be the completed G-operator Magnus algebra, with degree ideal I=(X0,X1). For a positive integer N and an open two-sided ideal J⊂Λ=Z2 [[G]], define the finite coefficient-degree quotientMN,J:=M/(IN+JM).Equivalently, keep words of degree <N, but reduce their Λ-coefficients modulo the finite ring Λ/J. There are only finitely many words of degree <N, and Λ/J is finite, so MN,J is finite.LetPN,J:=P∩(1+IN+JM).

Then PN,J is an open G-stable normal subgroup of P, andP/PN,J is finite. Moreover,N,J⋂PN,J=1,because the operator Magnus embedding is separated and the open ideals J exhaust the topology of Λ.

So the corrected finite approximation is not F/PN, butFN,J:=F/PN,J≅(P/PN,J)⋊G.The quotient still has the full G upstairs, but its pro-2 kernel is finite.

3. Correct finite solution sets Fix once and for all the relator orientation used in the sweep. Since rS and rS−1 generate the same closed normal subgroup, we may setρS:=rS−1 if that is the orientation matching the Turn 1 H1 -matrix, and requireΦN,J(ρS)=rH in FN,J.

Define SN,J to be the set of over-G automorphismsΦN,J:FN,J→FN,J such that:ΦN,J(ρS)=rH in FN,J, and the induced map on the first G-operator Magnus layer is the fixed invertible H1 -matrix from Turn 1.

Now SN,J is genuinely finite. An over-G automorphism is determined by finite data: the images of x0,x1 in the finite group P/PN,J, plus a section twist c:G→P/PN,J. Since G is topologically generated by σ,τ, a continuous cocycle into the finite group P/PN,J is determined by finitely many possible values c(σ),c(τ) satisfying the finite cocycle version of τσ=τ2. Thus only finitely many twists occur.

This is the precise finite replacement for Turn 20’s SN.

4. Nonemptiness of every SN,J The prior sweep supplies the ingredients.

Turn 1 produced an invertible abelianized Nielsen matrix overΛ=Z2 [[G]]carryingwS=(σ+3E,−2)towH=(Eg0+3E−2,σ−E).On the (1−E)-piece the rows swap; on the Ef-piece the matrix U0 has determinant s, a unit; and on E(1−f) the corresponding matrix is also invertible.

After this H1 -change, the history repeatedly reduces all nontrivial higher obstruction questions to the residual Ef, mod-2, s=1 Magnus equationS(Y0,Y1,q)=(Y1,Y1q)−1Y1−2Y03Y0q=X2.

Turn 18 then proves the residual obstruction tower vanishes to all orders: the induction givesCm=Lm (m≥5),so every even square quotient is hit by a root transgression W↦W[2], and every odd layer is an ordinary boundary.For a fixed finite pair (N,J), the correction problem is therefore a finite nilpotent Magnus problem over Λ/J. The non-residual pieces are solved by unit coefficients, and the residual obstruction is killed by the Turn 18 induction. HenceSN,J=∅ for every N,J.This is the corrected version of Turn 20’s nonemptiness claim. The argument is not “P/PN is finite”; it is “after degree and coefficient truncation, the finite problem has no obstruction.”

5. Compactness with the corrected inverse system

If (M,K) is a finer truncation than (N,J), meaning

M≥N,K⊆J,then reduction gives a well-defined mapρ(M,K),(N,J):SM,K→SN,J.

The sets SN,J are finite and nonempty. For any finite collection of indices (Ni,Ji), choose a common refinement (M,K), take an element of SM,K, and reduce it to all the smaller levels. Thus the inverse system has the finite-intersection property.

ThereforeN,JlimSN,J=∅.

Choose a compatible family(ΦN,J)N,J∈N,JlimSN,J.

Because

F≅N,JlimFN,J,the compatible finite-level automorphisms define a continuous over-G automorphism Φ:F→F.It is an automorphism, not merely an endomorphism, because each finite quotient map is an automorphism and the inverse maps are compatible as well.

For every N,J,Φ(ρS)rH−1∈PN,J.

Intersecting over all N,J givesΦ(ρS)=rH in F. Since ρS=rS±1, this implies Φ(⟨⟨rS⟩⟩F)=⟨⟨rH⟩⟩F.

Passing to quotients gives

F/⟨⟨rS⟩⟩F≅F/⟨⟨rH⟩⟩F,henceΓS≅ΓH.

Because every finite-level ΦN,J is over G, the limit Φ is also over G. Therefore the induced quotient isomorphism satisfiesπH∘Φ=πS.

So the final conclusion remainsΓS≅GΓH.

What changed from Turn 20

Turn 20’s compactness idea was right, but its finite-level model was too coarse. The degree-only quotients P/PN are generally infinite because the graded pieces carry Z2 [[G]]-coefficients. The correct finite objects are the coefficient-and-degree truncations P/PN,J.This repair is aligned with the structural lesson of the whole sweep: fixed finite gauges are unreliable, but full cofinal families of finite approximations are safe. Turn 20’s conclusion survives after replacing the N-tower by the directed (N,J)-tower.

ESTABLISHED: The compactness gluing proof is repaired. Replace the false claim “P/PN is finite” by the cofinal finite quotients P/PN,J, where both Magnus degree and Z2 [[G]]-coefficients are truncated. The corrected finite solution sets SN,J are finite, nonempty, compatible under reduction, and have a nonempty inverse limit. The limit over-G automorphism carries the closed normal closure of rS to that of rH, so ΓS≅GΓH.

GAP: No new mathematical obstruction appears, assuming the finite residual computations and the Turn 18 all-order residual induction are accepted. The remaining write-up gap is to state the operator Magnus embedding and the cofinal subgroups PN,J explicitly in the final proof.

NEXT: Write the final note using the corrected two-parameter compactness system: define M, I, J, PN,J, SN,J; prove nonemptiness from the residual induction; pass to the inverse limit; conclude ΓS≅GΓH.[TURN21-COEFFICIENT-TRUNCATED-COMPACTNESS-REPAIR]
